event: Judith holding the severed head of Holofernes
symbols: severed head on a platter — the defeat of the enemy by divine empowerment through a woman
sword — the instrument of judgment and divine justice
Judith's composed expression — the calm courage of faith in God's deliverance
laurel or flowering branch — victory and triumph
forest setting — the secrecy and danger of Judith's mission
description: A tall, narrow composition showing a beautiful young woman (Judith) standing among trees, facing the viewer with a calm, direct gaze. She wears a white draped garment with her dark hair adorned with flowers. In her arms she cradles a platter bearing the bearded severed head of a man (Holofernes), whose eyes are closed. In her other hand she holds a sword. Foliage and tree trunks surround her. The image is rendered in muted tones with delicate line work, appearing to be a print or watercolor drawing.
theological_description: Judith's beheading of the Assyrian general Holofernes is one of the most dramatic acts of deliverance in the deuterocanonical literature. When the Assyrian army besieged the Israelite city of Bethulia, Judith — a devout and beautiful widow — entered the enemy camp and, through cunning and courage empowered by prayer, slew the general in his own tent (Judith 13:6-8). Her act embodies the biblical pattern of God using the weak and unexpected to overthrow the powerful. This theme resonates throughout canonical Scripture: Jael driving a tent peg through Sisera's temple (Judges 4:21), David defeating Goliath with a sling (1 Samuel 17:50), and the Magnificat's declaration that God 'has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate' (Luke 1:52). Judith's composed beauty and deadly resolve together express the paradox that true strength often wears the appearance of weakness — a theology Paul articulates: 'God chose the weak things of the world to shame the strong' (1 Corinthians 1:27). Her prayer before the act — that God would 'crush their arrogance by the hand of a woman' — makes explicit that the victory belongs to the Lord.

scripture_refs.reason: Jael killing Sisera — the Old Testament archetype of a woman defeating Israel's enemy
God bringing down the mighty from their thrones — Judith's act as a fulfillment of this pattern
God choosing the weak to shame the strong — Judith as the humble instrument of divine victory
David defeating Goliath with unexpected means — the same pattern of unlikely victory through faith
